1: hoy ro'im ha-movdim umefitzim et-tzon mar'iti (hôy rōʿîm hamʾabĕdîm ûmĕpîṣîm ʾet-ṣōʾn marʿîtî): 'Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture!' The hoy (woe) oracle against the shepherds. Ro'im (shepherds) is the standard prophetic metaphor for political and religious leaders (cf. Ezek 34; Zech 11; John 10:11-16). Mar'iti (my pasture) — the possessive pronoun makes the sheep YHWH's directly: the shepherds are stewards, not owners. Movdim (destroying) and mefitzim (scattering) are the two failures: the leaders both destroy by active harm and scatter by passive neglect. The 'pasture' image connects to Ps 23 and Ezek 34's divine shepherd counterpart.
2: atem hafitzothem et-tzoni vatdachu otam velo feqadtem otam (ʾattem hăpîṣôtem ʾet-ṣōʾnî wattiddĕḥû ʾôtām wĕlōʾ pĕqadtem ʾôtām): 'You have scattered my flock and driven them away, and have not cared for them.' The double accusation uses nadach (drive away forcibly) and the negation of paqad (attend to/care for). Paqad is the comprehensive verb for YHWH's covenantal attention: to visit in blessing (Gen 21:1: 'the LORD visited Sarah'), to call to account in judgment (Exod 20:5: 'visiting the iniquity'), to appoint to a task (Num 3:10). The leaders have not 'paqad'-ed the flock: no attention, no care, no accountability. So YHWH will now paqad the evil of their deeds upon them — the same verb in both accusation and punishment.
3: va'ani aqbetz et-she'erit tzoni mikkol ha'aratzot asher hidachti otam sham: 'I myself will gather the remnant of my flock from all the lands where I have driven them, bring them back to their pasture, and they will be fruitful and multiply.' The pronoun va'ani (I myself / and I) emphasizes that YHWH directly takes on the task the failed shepherds abandoned. The remnant (she'erit) gathered from all lands is the core of the restoration promise. Paru veravvu (fruitful and multiply) echoes Gen 1:28 and 9:1 — the re-creation formula signals that the gathering of exiles is a new creation act. Jesus's sending of the twelve (Matt 10:6) and his claim to be the Good Shepherd who gathers the lost sheep of the house of Israel (Matt 15:24; John 10:16) enacts this promise personally.
4: vahaqimoti aleichem ro'im varou otam (wahăqimōtî ʿălêkem rōʿîm ûrāʿû ʾôtām): 'I will raise up shepherds who will truly care for them; they will no longer be afraid or terrified, and none will be lost.' The promise of proper shepherds (ro'im) is the positive counterpart to the woe-oracle against the bad shepherds. Lo yira'u velo yechatu velo yipaqedu (no longer afraid, terrified, or missing/lost) — three negations that reverse the flock's current state of fear, panic, and loss. Lo yipaqedu uses the paqad root again: none will be 'lacking' in the sense of unaccounted for. This anticipates the parable of the lost sheep (Luke 15:3-7): the good shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to find the one that is missing.
5: hineh yamim baim neum YHWH vahaqimoti leDavid tzemach tzaddiq (hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum Yhwh wăhăqîmōtî lĕdāwid ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq): 'The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch.' Tzemach (branch/shoot/sprout) is the technical messianic title used in Zech 3:8 ('my servant the Branch'), Zech 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch'), and Jer 33:15 (repetition of this verse). Tzaddiq (righteous) — the quality the current kings lacked (ch. 22 throughout). The Branch raised up 'for David' (leDavid) fulfills the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12: 'I will raise up your offspring after you') after the covenant-line kings have all failed. Melek vemaslach uasah mishpat utzedaqah ba-aretz (reign as king, act wisely, establish justice and righteousness in the land) — the accumulated demands of all the failed kings (22:3, 15) are fulfilled by this one figure.
6: YHWH Tzidqenu (Yhwh ṣidqênû): 'The LORD Our Righteousness.' This is the messianic name — the most Christologically direct name in Jeremiah. The name does two things at once: (1) it identifies the Branch as YHWH himself present in human form (the divine name YHWH is part of the messianic title); (2) it identifies the covenant problem solved: tzidqenu (our righteousness) means the righteousness the covenant required but Israel could never provide is now provided by YHWH himself in the person of his anointed. Paul's formulation — 'God made him who knew no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21) and 'Christ Jesus, who has become our wisdom, our righteousness, our holiness and our redemption' (1 Cor 1:30) — are the explicit NT appropriation of this messianic name. The name also appears again at Jer 33:16 where, remarkably, it is given to Jerusalem itself — the community named after its king's character.
7: lachen hineh yamim baim neum YHWH velo-yomru od chai YHWH asher he'elah et-benei Yisrael me'eretz Mitzraim: 'So the days are coming when no one will say any longer, 'As the LORD lives who brought the Israelites up from the land of Egypt.'' The eschatological new-exodus supercedes the old Exodus as the defining saving act. The oath formula 'as the LORD lives who brought Israel from Egypt' was the standard covenantal oath (cf. 16:14-15, an almost identical passage). The replacement of the foundational exodus narrative with a greater one is a bold claim: the future redemption will be so vastly greater that the Exodus — the paradigmatic act of salvation — will recede in comparison. Paul's 'once a slave, now a son' language (Gal 4:3-7) and the entire Letter to the Hebrews (Heb 3-4: a greater Moses, a greater rest) develops this Jeremianic logic.
8: ki im-chai YHWH asher he'elah ve'asher hevi et-zera beit Yisrael me'eretz tzafon: 'But, 'As the LORD lives who brought up the descendants of Israel from the north country.'' The north country (eretz tzafon) is Babylon — the new defining place of captivity and liberation. Just as the Exodus defined YHWH as the God who rescues from Egypt, the new exodus will define him as the God who brings back from Babylon and from all the dispersed lands. The universal scope — 'and from all the countries where he had driven them' — expands beyond the historical Babylonian exile to include all diasporas.
9: lanevi'im nishbar libi veqirbi kol-atzmotai raafa (lannĕbiʾîm nišbar libbî wĕqirbî kol-ʿaṣmôtay rāʿăpû): 'My heart is broken within me; all my bones tremble.' This is one of the most raw expressions of prophetic pathos in Scripture. The 'broken heart' (lev nishbar) — the same root as the 'broken and contrite heart' of Ps 51:17 — here is Jeremiah's heart breaking over the state of the prophetic community rather than his own sin. 'Like a drunken man, like a man overcome by wine' (kish avar yayin vekigever gabar bo yayin): the divine word overwhelms Jeremiah's capacity to stand firm, leaving him staggering. This uncontrolled response to the holy word is parallel to Daniel's falling on his face (Dan 8:17), John's falling as dead (Rev 1:17), and Isaiah's undone response to the vision (Isa 6:5).
10: ki-mealeifem nina'afu ha-aretz avlu (kî-mĕʾalĕpîm niʾăpāh hāʾāreṣ ʾāḇĕlāh): 'For the land is full of adulterers. Because of the curse the land mourns.' Ninafu (full of adulterers) — both literal sexual unfaithfulness and the prophetic metaphor for idolatry (cf. Hos 4:2: 'there is swearing, lying, murder, stealing, committing adultery; they break all bounds'). The land itself mourns (avlah) in response — creation sympathetically grieving the covenant violation (cf. Rom 8:22: 'the whole creation has been groaning'). The phrase 'the wilderness pastures are dried up' (yavshu ne'ot midbar) connects moral failure to ecological consequence: the land's fertility depends on covenant fidelity.
11: gam navi gam-kohen chanfu (gam-nābîʾ gam-kōhēn ḥānēpû): 'Both prophet and priest are godless; even in my own house I have found their wickedness.' Chaneph (godless/profane/corrupt) carries the sense of defilement through transgression — the breaking of sacred boundaries. The house of YHWH (beiti, the temple itself) has been defiled by prophetic and priestly corruption — the sacred space is no longer protected by its designated guardians. Ezekiel's temple-vision (Ezek 8) provides the detailed picture of what this corruption looked like.
12: lachen yihyeh darkam lahem kehalaqlaqqot ba-afelah (lākēn yihyeh darkām lāhem kaḥălaqlaqqôt bāʾăpēlāh): 'Therefore their path will be like slippery ground in the darkness; they will be driven stumbling along it and fall.' Chalaqlaqqot (slippery places) — the same image as Ps 35:6 ('let their path be dark and slippery') for divine judgment on the wicked. The 'year of their reckoning' (shenat peqadatam) uses the paqad root: the year when YHWH will visit them as he promised. The reckoning falls on the year the punishment arrives — not arbitrary but scheduled in the divine economy.
13: uvnevi'ei Shomeron ra'iti tiflet (ûbnĕbîʾê šōmĕrôn rāʾîtî tiplet): 'In the prophets of Samaria I saw a repulsive thing: they prophesied by Baal and led my people Israel astray.' The comparison structure (Samaria = bad; Jerusalem = worse) is a rhetorical intensification. Baal-prophets in Samaria led Israel into explicit idolatry — the offense is clear. But the Jerusalem prophets' sin is greater because it is more subtle and more spiritually lethal: they use YHWH's name for their lies.
14: uvnevi'ei Yerushalaim ra'iti sha'aruriyah na'of vehalokh vashaqer (ûbnĕbîʾê yĕrûšālaim rāʾîtî šaʿărûrîyāh niʾôp wĕhālôk baššāqer): 'But in the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen something even more horrible: they commit adultery and live by lies; they encourage evildoers so that no one turns from his evil.' Sha'aruriyah (horrifying/shocking thing) is stronger than tiflet (repulsive, v. 13) — Jerusalem is worse. The specific sins: adultery (niuf), lies (shaqer), and the pastoral failure of encouraging evildoers (hezeq yedei mere'im) so that no one repents. The last item is the most serious: pastoral corruption that prevents repentance is corruption at the most fundamental level. Hayyu li kulam kesodom veyosheveiha ke'amorah (they have all become to me like Sodom, their inhabitants like Gomorrah) — the ultimate prophetic judgment, since Sodom and Gomorrah were destroyed without remnant.
15: lachen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot al-hanevi'im hineni maachilem laanah: 'Therefore thus says the LORD of hosts concerning the prophets: I am going to feed them wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink.' La'anah (wormwood, a bitter desert plant) and mei rosh (water of poison/gall) are images of bitter judgment (cf. Lam 3:15, 19; Amos 5:7; Rev 8:11). The poisoned feeding is measure-for-measure: they fed the people with false spiritual food ('false hopes', v. 16); YHWH feeds them with bitterness. The spread of ungodliness (chanuppah) from Jerusalem's prophets throughout the land is the ecological disaster of false prophecy.
16: al-tishme'u el-divrei hanevi'im hanivi'im lachem mahbilim atem et-hazon libam yedaberu: 'Do not listen to the prophets who are prophesying to you. They are filling you with false hopes; they speak visions from their own imagination, not from the mouth of the LORD.' Mahbilim (filling with false hopes / making vain) from hevel (vanity/breath/nothing — Ecclesiastes' signature word). Chazon libam (visions of their own heart/mind) is the diagnosis: they prophesy from their own imagination, not from divine revelation. The criterion for authenticity is 'from the mouth of the LORD' (mipi YHWH) — the standard that Deut 18:18-19 established for the true prophet-like-Moses.
17: omrim lemaneatzei dvar YHWH shalom yihyeh lachem (ʾōmĕrîm lĕmĕnaʾăṣê dĕbar Yhwh šālôm yihyeh lākem): 'They keep telling those who follow the stubbornness of their own heart, 'No disaster will come upon you.'' The false prophets are pastoral parasites: they tell those who despise the word of YHWH that all will be well. Menatze dvar YHWH (despises the word of YHWH) — the very people whose conduct requires confrontation are promised comfort. The 'stubbornness of their own heart' (sherirut libam) is the diagnostic phrase recurring throughout Jeremiah (3:17; 7:24; 9:14; 11:8; 13:10; 16:12; 18:12) for the willful self-determination that rejects YHWH's direction.
18: ki mi amad besod YHWH vayar vayishma et-dvaro (kî mî ʿāmad bĕsôd Yhwh wĕyirʾ wĕyišmaʿ ʾet-dĕbārô): 'For which of them has stood in the LORD's council to see and hear his word?' Sod YHWH (the council/assembly/secret of YHWH) is the divine court — the heavenly assembly of YHWH surrounded by celestial beings, from which prophetic commissions are issued (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19-23 where Micaiah sees the divine court; Isa 6 where Isaiah is commissioned from the heavenly throne-room; Job 1-2 where the divine court is depicted). Standing in YHWH's council is the criterion that separates true prophets from false: genuine prophets receive their message from a direct divine commission within the heavenly court, while false prophets generate messages from their own hearts. Amos 3:7 states the same principle: 'Surely the LORD God does nothing without revealing his secret (sod) to his servants the prophets.'
19: hineh se'arat YHWH chemah yatza'ah usa'ar mitgolelel (hinnēh saʿărat Yhwh ḥēmāh yāṣĕʾāh wĕsaʿar miṯgôlēl): 'Behold, the storm of the LORD! His wrath has gone out, a swirling tempest; it will fall violently on the head of the wicked.' The divine storm-wind (se'arah) is the theophanic vehicle used in Ezekiel's inaugural vision (Ezek 1:4), Job's divine speech (Job 38:1), and Elijah's translation (2 Kgs 2:1, 11). Here it is specifically judicial — the storm of divine wrath ('on the head of the wicked') that the false prophets are failing to announce.
20: lo yashuv af-YHWH ad-asoto ve'ad-haqimo mezimot libo (lōʾ yāšûb ʾap-Yhwh ʿad-ʿăśōtô wĕʿad-hăqîmô mĕzimmôt libbô): 'The LORD's anger will not turn back until he has accomplished the purposes of his heart. In the latter days you will understand it clearly.' The phrase be'acharit hayamim (in the latter days / end of days) has both near-future (the exile) and eschatological senses. The understanding that will come 'in the latter days' is retrospective comprehension: what seems obscure now will be transparent after the judgment falls. Repeated at 30:24 as the bookend of the 'Book of Consolation.'
21: lo-shalach'ti et-haneviim vehem ratzu lo dibbarti aleyhem vehem niv'au (lōʾ-šālaḥtî ʾet-hannĕbiʾîm wĕhēm rāṣû lōʾ-dibbartî ʾălêhem wĕhēm nibbāʾû): 'I did not send these prophets, yet they went running; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied.' The double denial — 'I did not send ... I did not speak' — strips the false prophets of both authorization and content. The running without being sent (vayaratz) echoes Deut 18:20: 'the prophet who presumes to speak a word in my name that I have not commanded him to speak ... shall die.' The running enthusiasm of the false prophets is not a mark of authentic prophetic compulsion (cf. Amos 3:8: 'the lion has roared — who will not fear? The LORD God has spoken — who can but prophesy?') but of unauthorized self-promotion.
22: ve'im-amdu besodi veyashme'u et-devaray et-ami veshibum midarkam hara'ah: 'But if they had stood in my council and proclaimed my words to my people, they would have turned them from their evil ways and evil deeds.' The counterfactual reveals what authentic prophecy would have accomplished: actual repentance (shiv-um midarkam hara'ah, turning them from evil ways). False prophecy fails in its most fundamental purpose — it produces no change of direction. The measure of prophetic authenticity is not dramatic experience but productive effect: genuine divine words bring repentance; manufactured words cannot.
23: ha-elohei miqarov ani neum YHWH velo elohei merachok (hăʾĕlōhê miqqārōb ʾănî nĕʾum Yhwh wĕlōʾ ʾĕlōhê mērāḥōq): 'Am I only a God nearby, declares the LORD, and not a God far away?' The rhetorical question asserts divine omnipresence by denying its limitation. A 'God nearby' would be a local deity — a genius loci who presides over a specific location and could be evaded by leaving that space. The contrast is between a territorial deity (which the pagan gods were) and YHWH, who cannot be geographically escaped. The false prophets think they can tell their private 'dreams' without YHWH hearing — v. 23-24 dismantles that assumption.
24: im-yissater ish bamistorim va'ani lo er'enu neum YHWH halo et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz ani male: 'Can anyone hide in secret places where I cannot see him? ... Do I not fill heaven and earth?' Halo et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz ani male (do I not fill heaven and earth?): divine omnipresence stated as comprehensive spatial occupation. This is the OT's most direct statement of divine immensity — not merely that YHWH is present everywhere but that his presence fills the cosmos (cf. Ps 139:7-12; Isa 66:1; Acts 17:27-28: 'he is not far from each one of us ... in him we live and move and have our being'). The implication for false prophecy: YHWH hears every lie told in his name.
25: shama'ti et asher amru haneviim hanivi'im bishmi sheqer lemor chalamti chalamti: 'I have heard what the prophets are saying who prophesy lies in my name: 'I have dreamed, I have dreamed!'' Dreams as a vehicle of prophetic revelation had legitimate precedent (Gen 37; 1 Sam 28:6; Joel 2:28). The problem is not dreams per se but the use of dream-claims to manufacture authority for messages not received from YHWH. The doubling (chalamti chalamti, I have dreamed, I have dreamed) mimics the false enthusiasm of the prophets — the intensity of the claim inversely proportional to its authenticity.
26: ad-matai hayesh belev haneviim nivi'ei hashaqer unevei tarmiyt libam: 'How long shall there be lies in the heart of these prophets? They prophesy the deceit of their own hearts.' The ad-matai (how long?) is the lament formula used of prophetic endurance (4:21; Ps 6:3; 13:1-2). The diagnostic: lies originate in the lev (heart) — the false prophet's problem is not technique but orientation. A heart not aligned with YHWH generates messages aligned with its own desires.
27: hahoshvim lehashkiach et-ami shemi bechalomotam: 'They intend to make my people forget my name through the dreams they tell one another, just as their fathers forgot my name for Baal.' The naming here is precise: the goal of false prophecy (whether consciously intended or not) is to displace YHWH's name with something else. In the fathers' generation the displacement was explicit (Baal worship); in Jeremiah's generation it is happening through false prophecy — replacing the davar YHWH (actual word of YHWH) with manufactured dreams that serve human agendas.
28: hanavi asher itto chalom yesaper chalom va'asher dvari itto yedaber dvari emet: 'Let the prophet who has a dream tell the dream — but let the one who has my word speak my word faithfully. What does straw have to do with wheat?' The straw/wheat contrast is a refinery metaphor: threshing separates the worthless husk from the valuable grain. The dream may be real, but it is straw relative to the word of YHWH. Both may exist, but they are not equivalent. Emet (faithfully/truthfully) — the standard of transmission: even if the message is genuine, it must be transmitted without distortion.
29: halo koh dvari ka-esh neum YHWH vekhapatish yefatetz sela (hălōʾ kōh dĕbārî kāʾēš nĕʾum Yhwh wĕkĕpaṭṭîš yĕpōṣēṣ sālaʿ): 'Is not my word like fire, declares the LORD, and like a hammer that shatters rock?' Two images of irresistible destructive power: esh (fire, which consumes without leaving anything) and paratish (a heavy hammer that shatters rock). Both are applied to the divine word — making it causally effective, not merely informational. The hammer/rock image anticipates Matt 7:24-25 where the wise man builds on rock: the word that shatters rock for the unrepentant is the same word that provides foundation for the responsive. Heb 4:12 ('the word of God is living and active ... sharper than any two-edged sword') develops the same image of the word's penetrating power.
30: lachen hineni al-haneviim neum YHWH megannevi divrai ish mere'ehu: 'Therefore, I am against the prophets, declares the LORD, who steal my words from each other.' The triple hineni al (I am against, vv. 30-32) is a formal declaration of divine opposition used in judgment oracles against nations (cf. Ezek 26:3; 28:22; 29:3). 'Stealing words from each other' — the false prophets plagiarize each other's messages, recycling visions and dreams as their own without any actual divine encounter. The professionalization of prophecy has become an echo chamber of borrowed falsehoods.
31: hineni al-haneviim neum YHWH halosheim leshonam vayinneum ne'um: 'I am against the prophets, declares the LORD, who wag their own tongues and say, 'This is what the LORD declares.'' Leqach leshono (to take his tongue) — the false prophets use the messenger formula (ne'um YHWH) as a mechanical stamp, applying it to messages that originated in their own tongues, not YHWH's commission. The abuse of the prophetic formula simultaneously claims divine authority and denies it by attaching it to human invention.
32: hineni al-nivei chalomot shaqer neum YHWH vayesaprum vayat'u et-ami: 'I am against those who prophesy lying dreams ... who lead my people astray with their lies and their recklessness, when I neither sent them nor commissioned them. They are of no benefit whatsoever.' Pachazutam (recklessness/wantonness) — the same root as Judges 9:4's 'reckless and irresponsible' men. The climax: 'of no benefit whatsoever' (lo-ho'el yo'ilu) — the double root for usefulness denied twice, the strongest possible negation. False prophecy does not merely fail to help; it actively harms by leading astray.
33: veki yisha'alcha ha'am hazeh o hanavi o hakohen lemor ma massa YHWH (wĕkî yišʾālĕkā hāʿam hazzeh ʾô hannābîʾ ʾô hakkōhēn lēmōr mah-massāʾ Yhwh): 'When this people, or a prophet or a priest, asks you, 'What is the burden of the LORD?' you shall say to them, 'You are the burden, and I will cast you off.'' The Hebrew wordplay: massa (burden/oracle) is the standard term for a prophetic pronouncement against the nations (cf. Isa 13:1: 'the oracle [massa] concerning Babylon'; Nah 1:1; Hab 1:1; Mal 1:1). But massa also literally means 'a burden to be carried.' Jeremiah exploits the double meaning: they ask for the prophetic oracle (massa), but YHWH says they themselves are the burden (massa) he is about to cast off (natashti). The oracle-seekers have become the oracle.
34: vehanavi vehakohen ve'ish asher yomar massa YHWH: 'As for the prophet, the priest, or any person who says 'The burden of the LORD,' I will punish that person and his household.' The punishment extends to the household — the familial unit that benefited from the prophet's profession. This was standard in ancient legal thinking: corporate household accountability (cf. Achan's family in Josh 7:24-25). The commercialization of prophecy corrupted the entire household economy built on it.
35: koh tomeru ish el-re'ehu ve'ish el-achiv meh anah YHWH umah diber YHWH: 'This is what you are to say to one another: 'What has the LORD answered?' or 'What has the LORD spoken?'' The replacement formula: instead of asking for the 'burden' (massa), the community is instructed to ask in straightforward factual terms: what did YHWH answer? what did YHWH say? These questions require specific content, not the oracular formula. The shift from massa YHWH to mah diber YHWH is the shift from prophetic brand to prophetic content.
36: ki et-massa YHWH tizkeru od ki yihyeh massa ish dvaro: 'You must no longer speak of 'the burden of the LORD,' for the burden is every person's own word, and you have perverted the words of the living God.' The logic: calling human words 'the burden of YHWH' is perverting (hafach, overturning/distorting) the words of the living God. Elohim chayim YHWH Tzvaot elohenu (the living God, the LORD of Armies, our God) — the full divine name stacked to emphasize the gravity of the perversion. Using YHWH's name for human speech is an ontological confusion, not merely a technical violation.
37: The instruction to the prophet at v. 37 repeats the formula of v. 35 but now addresses the prophet directly rather than the general community. The shift in address — from 'you to one another' to 'you to the prophet' — shows Jeremiah is providing laypeople with language to hold their prophetic authorities accountable. The same question — what has YHWH answered, what has YHWH spoken — applies to the prophets themselves.
38: ve'im massa YHWH tomeru lachen koh-amar YHWH ya'an emarchem et-haddavar hazeh massa YHWH: 'But if you say 'the burden of the LORD,' thus says the LORD: Because you have spoken these words — 'the burden of the LORD' — when I sent word to you that you were not to say 'the burden of the LORD.'' The specific disobedience is named: continuing to use the forbidden formula after being explicitly instructed not to. This transforms the formula itself into a test of obedience — using it after the prohibition is itself the violation.
39: lachen hineni venashiti etchem nasa (lākēn hinnĕnî wĕnāśîtî ʾetkem nāśōʾ): 'Therefore, behold, I will surely lift you up and fling you away from my presence.' The pun completes itself: they asked for the 'burden' (massa); YHWH will make them the 'lifted up and carried' (nasa) object — carried away and hurled from his presence. The covenant consequence of perverting divine speech is removal from the covenant space: exile from the city YHWH gave them and their fathers.
40: venatateti aleichem cherpat olam uvoshet olam asher lo tishkach (wĕnātattî ʿălêkem ḥerpat ʿôlām ûbôšet ʿôlām ʾăšer lōʾ tiššākēaḥ): 'And I will bring on you everlasting disgrace and lasting shame that will not be forgotten.' Cherpat olam (everlasting disgrace) and boshet olam (lasting shame) — the two shame-words paired with the qualifier olam (everlasting/perpetual) make the judgment permanent. Not merely temporal punishment but permanent memorial of the false prophets' corruption. The final phrase asher lo tishkach (that will not be forgotten) is the ultimate covenant-judgment: the positive 'I will remember their sin no more' of 31:34 is here inverted into permanent remembrance of shame.
